title: Fast Geometric Projections for Local Robustness Certification

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## FAST GEOMETRIC PROJECTIONS FOR LOCAL ROBUSTNESS CERTIFICATION

**Aymeric Fromherz** _[∗]_ Carnegie Mellon University Pittsburgh, PA, USA fromherz@cmu.edu

**Klas Leino** _[∗]_ Carnegie Mellon University Pittsburgh, PA, USA kleino@cs.cmu.edu

## **Matt Fredrikson**

Carnegie Mellon University Pittsburgh, PA, USA mfredrik@cmu.edu

**Bryan Parno** Carnegie Mellon University Pittsburgh, PA, USA parno@cmu.edu

## **Corina P˘as˘areanu**

Carnegie Mellon University and NASA Ames Moffett Field, CA, USA pcorina@cmu.edu

## ABSTRACT

Local robustness ensures that a model classifies all inputs within an _ℓp_ -ball consistently, which precludes various forms of adversarial inputs. In this paper, we present a fast procedure for checking local robustness in feed-forward neural networks with piecewise-linear activation functions. Such networks partition the input space into a set of convex polyhedral regions in which the network’s behavior is linear; hence, a systematic search for decision boundaries within the regions around a given input is sufficient for assessing robustness. Crucially, we show how the regions around a point can be analyzed using simple geometric projections, thus admitting an efficient, highly-parallel GPU implementation that excels particularly for the _ℓ_ 2 norm, where previous work has been less effective. Empirically we find this approach to be far more precise than many approximate verification approaches, while at the same time performing multiple orders of magnitude faster than complete verifiers, and scaling to much deeper networks. An implementation of our proposed algorithm is available on GitHub[1] .

## 1 INTRODUCTION

We consider the problem of verifying the _local robustness_ of piecewise-linear neural networks for a given _ℓp_ bound. Precisely, given a point, _x_ , network, _F_ , and norm bound, _ϵ_ , this entails determining whether Equation 1 holds.

This problem carries practical significance, as such networks have been extensively shown to be vulnerable to _adversarial examples_ (Papernot et al., 2016; Szegedy et al., 2014), wherein smallnorm perturbations are chosen to cause arbitrary misclassifications. Numerous solutions have been proposed to address variants of this problem. These can be roughly categorized into three groups: learning rules that aim for robustness on known training data (Croce et al., 2019; Madry et al., 2018; Wong & Kolter, 2018; Zhang et al., 2019; Xiao et al., 2019), post-processing methods that provide stochastic guarantees at inference time (Cohen et al., 2019; Lecuyer et al., 2018), and network verification (Balunovic et al., 2019; Cheng et al., 2017; Dutta et al., 2018; Ehlers, 2017; Fischetti & Jo, 2018; Gowal et al., 2018; Jordan et al., 2019; Katz et al., 2017; 2019; Singh et al., 2019b; Tjeng & Tedrake, 2017; Wang et al., 2018; Weng et al., 2018).
We focus on the problem of network verification—for a given model and input, determining if Equation 1 holds—particularly for the _ℓ_ 2 norm. Historically, the literature has primarily concentrated

on the _ℓ∞_ norm, with relatively little work on the _ℓ_ 2 norm; indeed, many of the best-scaling verification tools do not even support verification with respect to the _ℓ_ 2 norm. Nonetheless, the _ℓ_ 2 norm remains important to consider for “imperceptible” adversarial examples (Rony et al., 2019). Furthermore, compared to the _ℓ∞_ norm, efficient verification for the _ℓ_ 2 norm presents a particular challenge, as constraint-solving (commonly used in verification tools) in Euclidean space requires a non-linear objective function, and cannot make as effective use of interval-bound propagation.

Existing work on verifying local robustness for the _ℓ_ 2 norm falls into two primary categories: _(1)_ expensive, but exact decision procedures, e.g., GeoCert (Jordan et al., 2019) and MIP (Tjeng & Tedrake, 2017), or _(2)_ fast, but approximate techniques, e.g., FastLin/CROWN (Weng et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018). While approximate verification methods have shown promise in scaling to larger networks, they may introduce an additional penalty to robust accuracy by flagging non-adversarial points, thus limiting their application in practice. Exact methods impose no such penalty, but as they rely on expensive constraint-solving techniques, they often do not scale well to even networks with a few hundred neurons.

In this paper, we focus on bridging the gap between these two approaches. In particular, we present a verification technique for Equation 1 that neither relies on expensive constraint solving nor conservative over-approximation of the decision boundaries.

Our algorithm (Section 2) leverages simple projections, rather than constraint solving, to exhaustively search the model’s decision boundaries around a point. The performance benefits of this approach are substantial, especially in the case of _ℓ_ 2 robustness, where constraint solving is particularly expensive while Euclidean projections can be efficiently computed using the dot product and accelerated on GPU hardware. However, our approach is also applicable to other norms, including _ℓ∞_ (Section 3.3). Our algorithm is embarassingly parallel, and straightforward to implement with facilities for batching that are available in many popular ML libraries. Additionally, we show how the algorithm can be easily modified to find certified lower bounds for _ϵ_ , rather than verifying a given fixed value (Section 2.3).

Because our algorithm relies exclusively on projections, it may encounter scenarios in which there is evidence to suggest non-robust behavior, but the network’s exact boundaries cannot be conclusively determined without accounting for global constraints (Section 2, Figure 1b). In such cases, the algorithm will return unknown (though it would be possible to fall back on constraint solving). However, we prove that if the algorithm terminates with a robust decision, then the model satisfies Equation 1, and likewise if it returns not_robust, then an adversarial example exists (Theorem 1). Note that unlike prior work on approximate verification, our approach can often separate not_robust cases from unknown, providing a concrete adversarial example in the former. In this sense, the algorithm can be characterized as _sound_ but _incomplete_ , though our experiments show that in practice the algorithm typically comes to a decision.

We show that our implementation outperforms existing exact techniques (Jordan et al., 2019; Tjeng & Tedrake, 2017) by multiple orders of magnitude (Section 3.1, Table 2a and Section 3.3), while rarely being inconclusive on instances for which other techniques do not time out. Moreover, we find our approach enables _scaling to far deeper models_ than prior work — a key step towards verification of networks that are used in practice. Additionally, on models that have been regularized for efficient verification (Croce et al., 2019; Xiao et al., 2019), our technique performs even faster, and scales to much larger models — including convolutional networks — than could be verified using similar techniques (Section 3.1, Table 2a). Finally, we compare our work to approximate verification methods (Section 3.2). We find that while our implementation is not as fast as previous work on efficient lower-bound computation for large models (Weng et al., 2018), our certified lower bounds are consistently tighter, and in some cases minimal (Section 3.2, Table 2b).

## 2 ALGORITHM

In this section we give a high-level overview of our proposed algorithm, and present some implementation heuristics that improve its performance (Section 2.2). We also propose a variant (Section 2.3) to compute certified lower bounds of the robustness radius. Correctness proofs for all of the algorithms discussed in this section are provided in Appendix A. ## 2.1 THE BASIC FAST GEOMETRIC PROJECTIONS ALGORITHM

We propose the _Fast Geometric Projections_ (FGP) algorithm, which takes a model, _F_ , an input, _x_ , and a bound, _ϵ_ , and either proves that Equation 1 is satisfied (i.e., _ϵ_ -local robustness), finds an adversarial input at distance less than _ϵ_ from _x_ , or returns unknown. Our algorithm assumes _F_ ( _x_ ) = argmax _{f_ ( _x_ ) _}_ , where _f_ : R _[m] →_ R _[n]_ is the function computed by a neural network composed of linear transformations with ReLU activations (i.e., _f_ is a feed-forward ReLU network).
The algorithm relies on an analysis of all possible _activation regions_ around _x_ . An activation region is a maximal set of inputs having the same _activation pattern_ of ReLU nonlinearities.

Formally, let _fu_ ( _x_ ) denote the pre-activation value of neuron _u_ in network _F_ when evaluating _x_ . We say that neuron _u_ is _activated_ if _fu_ ( _x_ ) _≥_ 0. An _activation pattern_ , _A_ , is a Boolean function over neurons that characterizes whether each neuron is activated. Then the _activation region_ , _A_[¯] , associated with pattern _A_ is the set of inputs that realize _A_ : _A_[¯] := _{x | ∀u._ ( _fu_ ( _x_ ) _≥_ 0) _⇐⇒ A_ ( _u_ ) _}_ .

Because we assume that _F_ is a piecewise-linear composition of linear transformations and ReLU activations, we can associate the activation status _A_ ( _u_ ) of any neuron with a closed half-space of R _[m]_ (Jordan et al., 2019). The _activation constraint_ , _Cu_ , for neuron _u_ and pattern _A_ is the linear inequality _wu[T][x]_[ +] _[ b][u][≤]_[0][,][where] _[w][u]_[and] _[b][u]_[satisfy] _[∀][x][∈]_[R] _[m][.w] u[T][x]_[ +] _[ b][≤]_[0] _⇐⇒ A_ ( _u_ ). The coefficients, _wu_ are equal to the gradient of _fu_ with respect to its inputs, evaluated at a point in _A_[¯] . Crucially, because the gradient is the same at every point in _A_[¯] , the constraints can be computed _from the activation pattern alone_ via backpropagation. More details on this computation are given in Appendix B.

The intersection of these constraints yields the activation region _A_[¯] , and the facets of _A_[¯] correspond to the non-redundant constraints. The convexity of activation regions follows from this observation, as does the fact that the _decision boundaries_ are also linear constraints of the form _f_ ( _x_ ) _i ≥ f_ ( _x_ ) _j_ for classes _i_ and _j_ .

The FGP algorithm performs a search of all activation regions that might be at distance less than _ϵ_ from _x_ . We begin by analyzing the region, _A_[¯] 0, associated with the activation pattern of the input, _x_ , as follows.

First, we check to see if _A_[¯] 0 contains a decision boundary, _C_ , that is at distance less than _ϵ_ from _x_ ; if so, we take the projection, _p_ , from _x_ onto _C_ . Intuitively, the projection is an input satisfying _C_ that is at minimal distance from _x_ , i.e., _∀x[′] .C_ ( _x[′]_ ) = _⇒∥x − p∥≤∥x − x[′] ∥_ . Similarly we define the

distance, _d_ , from _x_ to a constraint or decision boundary, _C_ , as _d_ ( _x, C_ ) := min _x′_ : _C_ ( _x′_ ) _∥x − x[′] ∥_ , i.e., _d_ is the distance from _x_ to its projection onto _C_ . If _p_ does not have the same class as _x_ according to _F_ (or lies directly on the decision boundary between classes), then _p_ is an adversarial example. If _p_ has the same class as _x_ , it means that the projection to the decision boundary is outside of the activation region we are currently analyzing; however, this is not sufficient to conclude that _no_ point on the decision boundary is inside both the current activation region and the _ϵ_ -ball (see Figure 1b). Therefore, we return unknown.

Otherwise, we collect all activation constraints in _A_[¯] 0; there is one such activation constraint per neuron _u_ in the network; thus each constraint corresponds to the neighboring region, _A_[¯] _[u]_ 0[, which has] the same activation pattern as _A_[¯] 0, except with neuron _u_ flipped, i.e., _A_ 0( _u[′]_ ) _̸_ = _A[u]_ 0[(] _[u][′]_[)] _[ ⇐⇒][u]_[ =] _[ u][′]_[.] For each constraint _Cu_ , we check whether it might be at distance less than _ϵ_ from _x_ using a geometric projection. Although _Cu_ is only valid in the polytope corresponding to the activation region _A_[¯] 0, we compute the distance to the hyperplane corresponding to the infinite prolongation of _Cu_ . As such, we only obtain a lower bound on the distance from _x_ to _Cu_ . If the computed distance is smaller than _ϵ_ , we enqueue the corresponding activation region if it has not been searched yet. Thus the queue contains unexplored regions that might be at distance less than _ϵ_ from _x_ . The algorithm continues to analyze each enqueued region in the same way, each explored region enqueuing neighbouring regions that might be at distance less than _ϵ_ from _x_ , until the queue is empty. Exhausting the queue means that we did not find any adversarial examples in any activation region that might intersect with the _ϵ_ -ball centered at _x_ . We therefore conclude that _x_ is locally _ϵ_ -robust. An illustration of a simple execution of this algorithm is shown in Figure 1a.

**Conditions on Adversarial Examples.** It is common in practice to include domain-specific conditions specifying which inputs are considered valid adversarial examples. For example, in the image domain, adversarial examples are typically subject to _box constraints_ requiring each pixel to be in the range [0 _,_ 1]. These constraints can easily be incorporated into the FGP algorithm by checking them when we check if _F_ ( _x_ ) _̸_ = _F_ ( _p_ ), when a decision boundary is found. If _p_ is not a valid adversarial example, the algorithm would return unknown.

**Correctness.** We show that when the FGP algorithm returns not_robust, there exists an adversarial example, and when it returns robust, the model is locally robust at _x_ , with radius _ϵ_ . However, the algorithm may also return unknown, in which case we do not claim anything about the robustness of the model. A complete proof of Theorem 1 is provided in Appendix A.1.

**Theorem 1.** _(1) When the FGP algorithm returns_ not ___ robust _, there exists an adversarial example, p, such that ∥x − p∥≤ ϵ and F_ ( _x_ ) _̸_ = _F_ ( _p_ ) _. (2) When it returns_ robust _, Equation 1 holds._

## 2.2 HEURISTICS FOR FGP

To improve the performance and precision of our algorithm, we use several additional heuristics.

**Batching.** The gradient backpropagations for computing constraints and the dot product projections for calculating distances lend themselves well to batching on the GPU. In our implementation we dequeue multiple elements from the queue at once and calculate the constraints and constraint distances for the corresponding regions in parallel, leading to a speedup of up to 2x on large examples _∼_ for a well-chosen batch size ( 10-100).

**Caching Intermediate Computations.** We observe that activation constraints for the first layer are invariant across all regions, as there is no earlier neuron to depend on. Thus, we can precompute all such constraints once for all inputs to be analyzed.

**Exploring the Full Queue.** If the analysis of a decision boundary is inconclusive, the FGP algorithm, as presented, stops and returns unknown. We also implement a variant where we instead record the presence of an ambiguous decision boundary but continue exploring other regions in the queue. If we empty the queue after unsuccessfully analyzing a decision boundary, we nevertheless return unknown. However, oftentimes a true adversarial example is found during the continued search, allowing us to return not_robust conclusively.

Table 1 displays the results of experiments evaluating the impact of exploring the entire search queue instead of stopping at the first decision boundary (as described in Section 2.1). ## 2.3 CERTIFIED LOWER BOUNDS

We now consider the related problem of finding a _lower bound_ on the local robustness of the network. A variant of the FGP algorithm can provide certified lower bounds by using a priority queue; constraints are enqueued with priority corresponding to their distance from _x_ , such that the closest constraint is always at the front of the queue. We keep track of the current certified lower bound in a variable, _β_ . At each iteration, we set _β_ to the maximum of the old _β_ and the distance from _x_ to the constraint at the front of the queue.

The algorithm terminates either when all constraints at distance less than the initially specified _ϵ_ were handled, or when a decision boundary for which the initial algorithm returns unknown or not_robust is found. In the first case, we return _ϵ_ ; in the second, we return the value stored in _β_ at this iteration.
The proof of this variant is similar to the proof of the FGP algorithm. It relies on the following loop invariant, which we prove in Appendix A.2.

**Loop Invariant 1.** _(1) All activation regions at distance less than β from x were previously visited, (2) β is always smaller than ϵ, and (3) there is no adversarial point at distance less than β._

## 3 EVALUATION

In this section, we evaluate the performance of our implementation of the FGP algorithm and its variant for computing lower bounds. Section 3.1 discusses the performance of FGP compared to existing tools that perform exact _ℓ_ 2 robustness verification. Section 3.2 compares the lower bounds certified by our FGP variant from Section 2.3 to those of other approximate certification methods. In short, we find that our approach outperforms existing exact verification tools by several orders of magnitude (2-4), and produces more precise lower bounds (3-25 times larger) than the relevant work on approximate certification.

Additionally, in Section 3.1, we explore the scalability of our algorithm. We find that when the networks are strongly regularized for verifiability, our approach even scales to CNNs. Finally, we remark on the flexibility of FGP with respect to the norm in Section 3.3, and observe that our approach is also faster than existing complete verification tools when performing _ℓ∞_ robustness verification.

We performed experiments on three standard datasets: MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and CIFAR10. We evaluated both on models trained for robustness using adversarial training (Madry et al., 2018), and on models trained for verifiability _and_ robustness using maximum margin regularization (MMR) (Croce et al., 2019) or ReLU Stability (RS) (Xiao et al., 2019). We refer to each model by the dataset it was trained on, followed by the architecture of the hidden layers. For example, “mnist20x3” refers to a model trained on MNIST with 3 hidden layers of 20 neurons each. The “cnn” architecture refers to a common CNN architecture used to benchmark CNN verification in the literature; details are given in Appendix C. Models marked with “*” were trained with MMR; models marked with “ _†_ ” were trained with RS; all other models were trained using PGD adversarial training (Madry et al., 2018). The hyperparameters used for training are given in Appendix C.

The upper bound on the VRA (VRAUB) is also given. Each instance is given a time budget of 120 seconds. In each experiment, measurements are obtained by evaluating on 100 arbitrary instances from the test set. All experiments were run on a 4.2GHz Intel Core i7-7700K with 32 GB of RAM, and a Tesla K80 GPU with 12 GB of RAM.

## 3.1 LOCAL ROBUSTNESS CERTIFICATION

We first compare the efficiency of our implementation to that of other tools certifying local robustness. GeoCert (Jordan et al., 2019) and MIP (Tjeng & Tedrake, 2017) are the tools most comparable to ours as they are able to exactly check for robustness with respect to the _ℓ_ 2 norm. We used commit hash 8730aba for GeoCert, and v0.2.1 for MIP. Specifically, we compare the median run time over the analyses of each of the selected 100 instances, the number of examples on which each tool terminates, the result of the analyses that terminate, and the corresponding verified robust accuracy (VRA), i.e., the fraction of points that were both correctly classified by the model and verified as robust. In addition, we report an upper bound on the VRA for each model, obtained by running PGD attacks (hyperparameters included in Appendix C) on the correctly-classified points on which every method either timed out or reported unknown. Results are given for an _ℓ_ 2 norm bound of _ϵ_ = 0 _._ 25, with a computation budget of two minutes per instance. The results for these experiments are presented in Table 2a.

We observe that FGP always outperforms GeoCert by two to three orders of magnitude, without sacrificing precision; i.e., we rarely return unknown when GeoCert terminates. MIP frequently times out with a time budget of 120 seconds, indicating that we are faster by at least four orders of magnitude. This is consistent with the 100 to 1000 seconds per solve on a MNIST model with three hidden layers of 20 neurons each reported by Tjeng & Tedrake (2017); their technique performs best on the _ℓ_ 1 or _ℓ∞_ norms. In addition, we find that FGP consistently verifies the highest fraction of points, and yields the best VRA. Moreover, on mnist20x3, FGP comes within 2 percentage points of the VRA upper bound. This suggests that FGP comes close to verifying _every_ robust instance on this model.

**Models Trained for Verifiability.** Adversarial training (Madry et al., 2018), used to train the models in Sections 3.1 and 3.2, attempts to ensure that points on the data manifold will be far from the model’s decision boundary. Even if it achieves this objective, it may nonetheless be difficult to verify because the performance of FGP depends not only on the decision boundaries, but also on the internal activation constraints. Thus, we expect certification will be efficient only when the points to be certified are also far from internal boundaries, leading to fewer regions to explore. Recent work has sought to develop training procedures that not only encourage robustness, but that also optimize

6

for efficient verification (Croce et al., 2019; Wong & Kolter, 2018; Xiao et al., 2019). Maximum margin regularization (MMR) (Croce et al., 2019) and ReLU stability (RS) (Xiao et al., 2019) are particularly suited to our work, as they follow from the same intuition highlighted above.

Using MMR, FGP is able to scale to much larger models with hundreds or thousands of neurons, and tens of layers, as shown in the bottom half of Table 2a. Here again, we see that FGP outperforms the other approaches in terms of both time and successfully-verified points. By comparison, while GeoCert also experienced improved performance on the MMR-trained models, our method remains over three orders of magnitude faster. MIP continued to time out in nearly every case.

We found that we were able provide even stronger regularization with RS, allowing us to scale to CNNs (Table 2a), which have far more internal neurons than even large dense networks. We found that these highly regularized CNNs verified more quickly than some of the less-regularized dense networks, though, as with other methods that produce verifiable CNNs, this came at the cost of a penalty on the model’s accuracy.

## 3.2 CERTIFIED LOWER BOUNDS

We now evaluate the variant of our algorithm computing certified lower bounds on the robustness radius (Section 2.3). To this end, we compare the performance of our approach to FastLin (Weng et al., 2018), which is designed to provide quick, but potentially loose lower bounds on the local robustness of large ReLU networks. We compare the certified lower bound reported by our implementation of FGP after 60 seconds of computation (on models large enough such that FGP rarely terminates in 60 seconds) to the lower bound reported by FastLin; FastLin always reported results in less than two seconds on the models analyzed. The results are presented in Table 2b. The mean lower bound is reported for both methods, and we observe that on the models tested, FGP is able to find a better lower bound on average, though it requires considerably more computation time.

Because the optimal bound may vary between instances, we also report the median ratio of the lower bounds obtained by the two methods on each individual instance. Here we see that FastLin may indeed be quite loose, as on a typical instance it achieves as low as 4% and only as high as 69% of the bound obtained by FGP. Finally, we note that when FGP terminates by finding a decision boundary, if the projection onto that boundary is a true adversarial example, then _the lower bound is tight_ . In our experiments, there were few such examples — three on fmnist500x4 and one on fmnist1000x3 — however, in these cases, the lower bound obtained by FastLin was very loose, achieving 4-15% of the optimal bound on fmnist500x4, and only 0.8% of the optimal bound on fmnist1000x3. This suggests that while FastLin has been demonstrated to scale to large networks, one must be careful with its application, as there may be cases in which the bound it provides is too conservative.

## 3.3 GENERALIZATION TO OTHER NORMS

The only aspect of FGP that depends on the norm is the projection and the projected distance computation, making our approach highly modular with respect to the norm. As such, we added support for _ℓ∞_ robustness certification by providing a projection and a projected distance computation in FGP.

In this section, we compare the efficiency of this implementation to GeoCert (Jordan et al., 2019) and ERAN (Singh et al., 2019a). ERAN uses abstract interpretation to analyze networks using conservative over-approximations. As such, the analysis can certify that an input is robust, but can yield false positives when flagging an input as not robust. In this evaluation, we use the _DeepPoly_ abstract domain, and the complete mode of ERAN which falls back on constraint solving when an input is classified as not robust.

Our experimental setup is similar to the one presented in Section 3.1. For each network, we arbitrarily pick 100 inputs from the test set and report the median analysis time of each tool. We give each tool a time budget of 120 seconds per input. We report the number of instances on which each tool terminates within this time frame, as well as the results of the analyses that terminate. Results are given in Table 3 for an _ℓ∞_ norm bound of _ϵ_ = 0 _._ 01, which contains roughly the same volume as the _ℓ_ 2 ball used in most of our evaluation.

For example, “mnist20x3” refers to a model trained on MNIST with 3 hidden layers of 20 neurons each. Models marked with an asterisk were trained with MMR (Croce et al., 2019); other models were trained using PGD adversarial training (Madry et al., 2018).

We observe that we consistently outperform GeoCert by about two orders of magnitude, both on models trained with MMR and using adversarial training. On models trained for robustness (upper rows of Table 3), FGP is 0.5x to 5x faster than ERAN. In particular, FGP seems to scale better to deeper networks, while ERAN performs better on wider networks. Interestingly, ERAN does not benefit as much from MMR training as FGP and GeoCert. On large models trained with MMR, our tool is one to two orders of magnitude faster than ERAN, and ERAN is only about 2x faster than GeoCert.

Finally, while FGP is able to determine robustness for almost all inputs deemed robust by GeoCert or ERAN, it struggles to find adversarial examples for non-robust points, which results in a higher number of unknown cases compared to the _ℓ_ 2 variant. This is not highly surprising, as projections are better-suited to the Euclidean space than to _ℓ∞_ space. While the projection in Euclidean space is unique, this is not always the case in _ℓ∞_ space; we pick one arbitrary projection which thus might not lead to an adversarial example.

## 4 RELATED WORK

Our work can be grouped with approaches for verifying neural networks that aim to check local robustness exactly (Jordan et al., 2019; Katz et al., 2017; 2019; Tjeng & Tedrake, 2017); the primary difference is that our approach avoids expensive constraint solving at the price of incompleteness.

GeoCert (Jordan et al., 2019) is the closest work to ours; it aims to exactly compute local robustness of deep neural networks for convex norms. Unlike our approach, GeoCert computes the largest _ℓp_ ball centered at an input point within which the network is robust. Our experimental comparison with GeoCert shows that our approach scales much better. This is not surprising as GeoCert relies on projections to polytopes, which are solved by a quadratic program (QP) with linear constraints. This enables to compute exactly the distance from a point to an activation constraint, instead of an underapproximation as our approach does. In contrast, our approach uses projections to affine subspaces, which have a simpler, closed-form solution. Lim et al. (2020) builds on GeoCert, but reduces the number of activation regions to consider by exploring the network in a hierarchical manner. Although Lim et al. reduce the number of QPs required for verification, their variant still relies on constraint solving. While they improve on GeoCert by up to a single order of magnitude, our approach consistently outperforms GeoCert by 2-4 orders of magnitude. MIP (Tjeng & Tedrake, 2017) is an alternative to GeoCert based on mixed-integer programming; it also requires solving QPs for the _ℓ_ 2 norm. We could fall back on similar techniques to provide a slower, but complete variant of our algorithm when our projections cannot reach a conclusion about a decision boundary.

Reluplex (Katz et al., 2017) and its successor, Marabou (Katz et al., 2019) are complete verification tools based on SMT solving techniques. Unlike our approach, Reluplex and Marabou do not support the _ℓ_ 2 norm. AI2 (Gehr et al., 2018) and its successor, ERAN (Singh et al., 2019b), are based on abstract interpretation (Cousot & Cousot, 1977), using conservative over-approximations to perform

8

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

their analysis, which leads to false positives, i.e. robust inputs incorrectly classified as not robust. A mode of ERAN enables complete verification by falling back on a constraint solver when an input is classified as not robust; however this tool does not support the _ℓ_ 2 norm either.

FastLin (Weng et al., 2018) exploits the special structure of ReLU networks to efficiently compute lower bounds on minimal adversarial distortions. CROWN (Zhang et al., 2018) later expanded this to general activation functions. Although FastLin has been shown to be very scalable, our experiments indicate that the computed bounds may be imprecise.

Recently, a quite different approach has been proposed for robustness certification. Randomized Smoothing (Cohen et al., 2019; Lecuyer et al., 2018) is a post-processing technique that provides a stochastic robustness guarantee at inference time. This approach differs from our approach in that it _(1)_ modifies the predictions of the original model (increasing the complexity of making predictions), and _(2)_ provides a _probabilistic_ robustness guarantee that is quantified via a confidence bound. As such it provides an alternative set of costs and benefits as compared to static verification approaches. Its complexity also differs from that of FGP, as it is dependent primarily on the number of samples required to perform its post-processing of the model’s output. We find that in our experimental setup, achieving the same probabilistic guarantee as the experiments described in (Cohen et al., 2019) requires 10[5] samples, taking approximately 4.5 seconds per instance. Thus, for the models in our evaluation, FGP is on average faster or comparable in performance.

## 5 CONCLUSION

In this paper, we presented a novel approach for verifying the local robustness of networks with piecewise linear activation functions, that relies neither on constraint solving nor conservative overapproximations, but rather on geometric projections. While most existing tools focus on the _ℓ_ 1 and _ℓ∞_ norms, we provide an efficient, highly parallel implementation to certify _ℓ_ 2-robustness. Our implementation outperforms existing exact tools by multiple orders of magnitude, while empirically maintaining the same or better precision under a time constraint. Additionally, we show that our approach is particularly suited to scale up network verification to _deeper_ networks—a promising step towards verifying large, state-of-the-art models.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper presents a compelling approach to local robustness certification using geometric projections, offering significant speedups over existing exact verifiers while maintaining strong precision. Its GPU-parallel design and focus on the under-addressed $\ell_2$ norm represent a meaningful contribution to the verification community. The empirical results convincingly demonstrate scalability advantages, particularly on deeper and regularized networks.

Overview issues:

- Incomplete handling of activation region boundaries undermines completeness claims: The algorithm relies on projecting onto infinite hyperplanes derived from activation constraints, but only checks whether the projection lies within the $\epsilon$-ball—without verifying that it lies within the actual polyhedral region $\bar{A}$ (Figure 1b). This means that even if a constraint's infinite prolongation is within $\epsilon$, the true boundary may not intersect the $\epsilon$-ball. The paper acknowledges this in Section 2.1 and Figure 1b, but the treatment is insufficient. The 'unknown' outcome is not just due to decision boundaries, but also due to this fundamental geometric imprecision in region traversal. This undermines the claim of being a 'complete' search strategy around the input, as many enqueued regions may be spuriously added due to false proximity signals. A more rigorous filtering step—such as checking feasibility of the projection against all region-defining constraints—should be required before enqueueing neighboring regions.

- Lack of ablation studies on key scalability drivers: The paper claims superior scalability to deeper and wider networks, yet provides no ablation analysis isolating the effects of depth, width, regularization, or input dimensionality. For instance, the jump from mnist20x3 to mnist20x9 shows a 400x runtime increase, but it is unclear how much stems from depth versus combinatorial growth in activation patterns. Similarly, the dramatic improvement on MMR/RS-regularized models is noted, but not quantified in terms of reduced region count or constraint density. Without such analysis, the scalability claims remain correlational rather than mechanistic. Readers cannot determine whether FGP fundamentally scales better, or simply benefits more from regularization than competitors. This is critical for assessing generalizability beyond the tested architectures.

- Overstated performance advantage due to unrepresentative baselines: The comparison to GeoCert and MIP is valid, but omits more recent or optimized exact methods that may close the performance gap. For example, Marabou (Katz et al., 2019) supports $\ell_2$ via external solvers and includes pruning techniques, while newer works like β-CROWN (Wang et al., NeurIPS 2021) combine bound tightening with search. By benchmarking only against older exact tools, the paper exaggerates FGP’s speed advantage. Moreover, the $\ell_\infty$ comparison includes ERAN’s complete mode but does not clarify whether MIP or Reluplex were run in equivalent complete configurations. This selective baseline choice risks overstating the novelty of the speed-precision tradeoff.

- Unclear termination criteria for lower bound variant: Section 2.3 describes a priority-queue variant for computing certified lower bounds, but the termination condition is ambiguous. It states the algorithm stops "when all constraints at distance less than the initially specified $\epsilon$ were handled", yet it is not defined what "handled" means—whether a constraint is processed only when its region is fully explored, or merely when its distance is computed. Given that region exploration can spawn new constraints, this loop may never terminate if new closer constraints keep appearing. The loop invariant (Loop Invariant 1) assumes progress, but no argument is given that the number of constraints within $\beta$ is finite or that the algorithm avoids infinite descent. This raises correctness concerns for the lower bound computation, especially in high-dimensional spaces where region boundaries are dense.

- Insufficient justification for projection-based region exploration heuristic: The core heuristic—enqueue neighboring regions if the distance to their activation constraint's infinite hyperplane is less than $\epsilon$—is justified only by its simplicity and parallelizability. However, this can lead to exploring regions that are geometrically close in the hyperplane sense but topologically distant in activation space (e.g., requiring multiple neuron flips). The paper does not analyze the false positive rate of this heuristic or compare it to alternatives like bounded BFS in activation pattern Hamming space. In high dimensions, this could result in an explosion of irrelevant regions being explored, limiting practical scalability. A discussion of the heuristic's precision and its impact on search space size is necessary to validate the design choice.

- No ablation on dimensionality and regularization impact: The paper claims scalability to deeper and wider networks, especially under regularization like MMR and RS, but provides no ablation study isolating the effects of input dimensionality, network depth, width, or regularization strength on performance. For instance, the runtime jump from mnist20x3 to mnist20x9 is dramatic, but it is unclear how much is due to depth versus increased activation pattern complexity. Similarly, while FGP performs well on MMR/RS-regularized models, the paper does not quantify how much these training methods reduce the number of activation regions or constraint density near the input. To support its scalability claims, the paper should include a controlled study varying one architectural parameter at a time—e.g., fixing width while increasing depth, or comparing regularized vs. unregularized versions of the same architecture—and report region count, queue size, and runtime. Without this, the scalability narrative remains observational rather than explanatory.

- Missing concrete example of projection failure mode: The paper acknowledges in Figure 1b that projections onto infinite hyperplanes may yield points outside the true polyhedral region, leading to 'unknown' outcomes, but it does not provide a concrete numerical example where this occurs. A worked instance—say, on a small 2D network with two hidden neurons—where the algorithm enqueues a neighboring region based on a hyperplane distance <  but the actual activation boundary lies outside the -ball would help readers assess the practical severity of the approximation. Computing the exact distance to the region facet versus the hyperplane prolongation in such a case would clarify the gap between the lower bound used and the true geometry. This is standard in geometric verification papers and necessary to calibrate trust in the method's precision.

- No comparison to recovery of prior methods in special case: The paper positions FGP as a faster alternative to exact methods like GeoCert, but does not verify that FGP recovers the same robustness decisions in cases where both terminate. For example, on the mnist20x3 model, GeoCert verifies 82 points as robust and 9 as not_robust within the time limit, while FGP verifies 87 and 8. The paper should explicitly check whether the 5 additional robust cases verified by FGP are correct (i.e., not false positives) and whether the 1 discrepancy in not_robust cases stems from a true adversarial example missed by GeoCert or a false negative. Without this side-by-side validation on shared instances, the claim of matching precision is not fully substantiated, even if the algorithm is sound.

- Unvalidated  norm extension lacks empirical grounding: The paper claims FGP generalizes to  norms (Section 3.3) and reports results in Table 3, but the  projection is not uniquely defined and the implementation picks an arbitrary one. This raises concerns about reliability, especially since the method finds fewer adversarial examples and returns more 'unknown' cases than in the  setting. The paper should demonstrate on a small model that the chosen  projection strategy is effective—e.g., by comparing multiple projection rules or showing that the selected point is often adversarial when one exists. Otherwise, the extension feels tacked on without rigorous validation, weakening the claim of modularity across norms.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Correct the region exploration logic to ensure that only activation constraints whose actual polyhedral facets intersect the $\epsilon$-ball are considered for enqueueing, or explicitly account for the geometric gap in the algorithm's guarantees.

- Add ablation studies that isolate the impact of network depth, width, regularization strength, and input dimension on FGP's runtime and region count, to substantiate scalability claims.

- Include comparisons to more recent exact or hybrid verifiers (e.g., Marabou, β-CROWN) under equivalent settings to ensure the performance advantage is not an artifact of outdated baselines.

- Clarify and formally justify the termination conditions for the lower bound variant, including a proof that the number of relevant constraints is finite and the priority queue makes progress.

- Analyze the precision of the projection-based enqueueing heuristic, including empirical data on how many enqueued regions are ultimately found to be outside the $\epsilon$-ball, and discuss potential improvements.

comments.title: Incorrect claim about Marabou's ℓ₂ support
comments.quote: Reluplex (Katz et al., 2017) and its successor, Marabou (Katz et al., 2019) are complete verification tools based on SMT solving techniques. Unlike our approach, Reluplex and Marabou do not support the  $\ell_2$  norm.
comments.explanation: The claim that Marabou does not support the $\ell_2$ norm is incorrect. Marabou integrates with external solvers such as Gurobi and can handle quadratic objectives required for $\ell_2$-distance minimization, enabling $\ell_2$-based robustness verification. This misrepresentation understates the capabilities of a key competitor and weakens the paper’s motivation for proposing a new method. The sentence should be corrected to reflect that Marabou supports $\ell_2$ via external solvers, distinguishing it from Reluplex.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of ERAN's ℓ₂ capabilities
comments.quote: A mode of ERAN enables complete verification by falling back on a constraint solver when an input is classified as not robust; however this tool does not support the $\ell_{2}$ norm either.
comments.explanation: This statement is factually inaccurate. ERAN supports $\ell_2$ robustness certification through integration with MIP solvers and other backends capable of handling quadratic constraints. It has been used in $\ell_2$-focused studies and benchmarks, contradicting the claim of no support. Given that the paper compares against ERAN in experiments, this error undermines the validity of the comparison and misleads readers about the landscape of available tools. The claim should be revised or removed to accurately reflect ERAN’s capabilities.

comments.title: Ambiguous 'handled' in termination condition
comments.quote: The algorithm terminates either when all constraints at distance less than the initially specified  $\epsilon$  were handled, or when a decision boundary for which the initial algorithm returns unknown or not_robust is found.
comments.explanation: The term 'handled' is not formally defined, creating ambiguity about when the algorithm terminates. Without specifying that a constraint is 'handled' only after being dequeued, processed, and its neighboring regions explored, it is unclear whether the search is complete within the $\epsilon$-ball. This affects the soundness of the certified lower bound. The sentence should be rewritten to use precise queue semantics: 'when no unprocessed constraint within distance $\epsilon$ remains in the queue' to ensure clarity and correctness.

comments.title: Incorrect return value overstates certified bound
comments.quote: In the first case, we return  $\epsilon$ ; in the second, we return the value stored in  $\beta$  at this iteration.
comments.explanation: Returning $\epsilon$ when all constraints within $\epsilon$ are handled falsely claims robustness up to that radius, even if $\beta < \epsilon$. The variable $\beta$ tracks the largest radius for which all closer regions have been safely explored. Since no guarantees exist beyond $\beta$, returning $\epsilon$ would be unsound. The correct return value in both cases is $\beta$, which represents the actual certified lower bound. This error undermines the correctness of the lower-bound variant and must be corrected.

comments.title: Loop invariant lacks progress guarantee
comments.quote: Loop Invariant 1. (1) All activation regions at distance less than  $\beta$  from  $x$  were previously visited, (2)  $\beta$  is always smaller than  $\epsilon$ , and (3) there is no adversarial point at distance less than  $\beta$ .
comments.explanation: The loop invariant ensures safety and coverage up to $\beta$, but does not guarantee progress. If multiple constraints lie at the same minimal distance, $\beta$ may not increase between iterations, risking non-termination even with finite constraints. The algorithm needs a mechanism to ensure that each iteration processes a new constraint, making forward progress. The invariant should be supplemented with a statement that each step removes a constraint from the queue permanently, ensuring finite termination under realistic assumptions.

comments.title: Loop invariant does not account for regions at exactly β
comments.quote: Loop Invariant 1. (1) All activation regions at distance less than  $\beta$  from  $x$  were previously visited, (2)  $\beta$  is always smaller than  $\epsilon$ , and (3) there is no adversarial point at distance less than  $\beta$ .
comments.explanation: The invariant only covers regions at distance strictly less than $\beta$, but $\beta$ is updated to the distance of the current constraint, which may define a boundary at exactly $\beta$. An adversarial region could start precisely at that distance and remain undetected. To ensure correctness, the invariant must include coverage up to $\beta$, or explicitly state that all constraints at distance $\leq \beta$ have been processed. Otherwise, the certified bound may exclude valid adversarial examples at the boundary.

comments.title: No guarantee of finite constraint generation within ε-ball
comments.quote: The algorithm terminates either when all constraints at distance less than the initially specified  $\epsilon$  were handled, or when a decision boundary for which the initial algorithm returns unknown or not_robust is found.
comments.explanation: The termination argument assumes only finitely many activation boundaries intersect the $\epsilon$-ball, but this is not justified. In deep ReLU networks, region boundaries can accumulate densely near a point, potentially leading to infinite constraints within a bounded neighborhood. Without assuming standard regularity conditions (e.g., non-degenerate weights), the algorithm may not terminate. The paper should explicitly state that finite intersection is assumed under typical network configurations to ground the theoretical analysis.

comments.title: Mismatch in reported VRA and R/NR counts
comments.quote: Table 2: (a) Comparison of  $\ell_2$  local robustness certification (FGP vs. GeoCert vs. MIP) on 100 arbitrary test instances, including the median runtime, the certification result — either robust (R), not_robust (NR), unknown (U), or a timeout (TO) — and the corresponding Verified robust accuracy (VRA).
comments.explanation: The reported VRA values do not match the robust (R) counts. For example, on mnist20x3, FGP verifies 87 robust instances, implying VRA = 0.87, but 0.82 is reported. Similar discrepancies appear across rows. Since VRA is defined as the fraction of correctly classified and verified robust points, and the test set has 100 instances, these numbers should align. Either the R count includes unverified cases or there is an arithmetic error. The VRA column must be recalculated or the definition clarified to resolve this inconsistency.

comments.title: Incorrect median ratio interpretation in Table 2(b)
comments.quote:
|  Model | FGPLB Mean Bound | FastLin Mean Bound | Median Ratio  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  fmnist500x4 | 0.124 | 0.078 | 0.329  |
|  fmnist500x5 | 0.134 | 0.092 | 0.693  |
|  fmnist1000x3 | 0.083 | 0.021 | 0.035  |
comments.explanation: The 'Median Ratio' values are numerically inconsistent with the preceding columns. If it represents FGPLB / FastLin, the values should be ~1.59, 1.46, and 3.95, not 0.329, 0.693, and 0.035. If it is FastLin / FGPLB, they should be ~0.629, 0.687, and 0.253—still not matching. The values suggest a different computation, possibly involving differences or minima. Given the text claims FGP yields '3-25 times larger' bounds, the ratio should exceed 1. The column must be relabeled or recomputed to reflect its true meaning.

comments.title: Missing support for GeoCert on CNN contradicts scalability claim
comments.explanation: The 'not supported' entry for GeoCert on cifar-cnn† invalidates the comparative speed claim for CNNs. The paper states FGP 'outperforms existing exact tools by several orders of magnitude', but if GeoCert does not support CNNs, no such comparison is possible. This makes the scalability claim unverified for convolutional architectures. The paper should either cite a CNN-capable version of GeoCert or qualify the claim to apply only to fully connected networks.

comments.title: Volume equivalence claim between ℓ₂ and ℓ∞ balls is unjustified
comments.quote: Results are given in Table 3 for an $\ell_{\infty}$ norm bound of $\epsilon=0.01$, which contains roughly the same volume as the $\ell_{2}$ ball used in most of our evaluation.
comments.explanation: The claim that an $\ell_\infty$ ball of radius 0.01 has roughly the same volume as an $\ell_2$ ball of radius 0.25 is mathematically implausible in high dimensions. For MNIST (n=784), the $\ell_\infty$ volume is $(0.02)^{784}$, while the $\ell_2$ volume scales with $(0.25)^{784}$ times a subexponential factor—orders of magnitude larger. The two settings are not comparable, and the equivalence statement misleads readers about evaluation difficulty. The claim should be removed or replaced with actual volume calculations.

comments.title: Inconsistent time budget compared to other sections
comments.quote: We compare the certified lower bound reported by our implementation of FGP after 60 seconds of computation (on models large enough such that FGP rarely terminates in 60 seconds) to the lower bound reported by FastLin; FastLin always reported results in less than two seconds on the models analyzed.
comments.explanation: The 60-second budget for FGP in Section 3.2 conflicts with the 120-second budget used elsewhere. This inconsistency biases the comparison against FGP, as the method may yield better bounds with additional time. To ensure fair evaluation, the same time limit should be used across all experiments. If 60 seconds was intentional, it must be justified and aligned with the rest of the paper’s evaluation framework.

comments.title: Unclear meaning of 'optimal bound' in comparison
comments.quote: In our experiments, there were few such examples — three on fmnist500x4 and one on fmnist1000x3 — however, in these cases, the lower bound obtained by FastLin was very loose, achieving 4-15% of the optimal bound on fmnist500x4, and only 0.8% of the optimal bound on fmnist1000x3.
comments.explanation: The term 'optimal bound' is misleading. The distance to the first decision boundary found by FGP is not necessarily the true minimal adversarial perturbation—it depends on search order and may miss closer boundaries in unexplored regions. Calling it 'optimal' overstates the result. The comparison should refer to it as 'FGP's certified lower bound' or 'achieved bound' to avoid implying global optimality.

comments.title: Unsupported claim of no precision sacrifice
comments.quote: We observe that FGP always outperforms GeoCert by two to three orders of magnitude, without sacrificing precision; i.e., we rarely return unknown when GeoCert terminates.
comments.explanation: The claim of 'no sacrificing precision' relies only on 'unknown' rates, not on agreement in robustness decisions. Without verifying that FGP and GeoCert agree on R/NR outcomes for shared terminating instances, the assertion lacks support. It is possible for FGP to misclassify robust points as not_robust or vice versa. The claim should be revised to 'with comparable rates of unknown outcomes' unless direct outcome alignment is provided.

comments.title: Misleading tightness claim without verification
comments.quote: Finally, we note that when FGP terminates by finding a decision boundary, if the projection onto that boundary is a true adversarial example, then *the lower bound is tight*.
comments.explanation: The tightness claim assumes the projection lies within the true polyhedral region, but the paper does not verify this. As shown in Figure 1b, projections onto infinite hyperplanes may fall outside the actual activation region, making the point invalid. Without confirming feasibility against all constraints of the neighboring region, the 'tightness' assertion is unsubstantiated. The authors must demonstrate that the projected point lies within both the $\epsilon$-ball and the adjacent region to support this claim.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
The paper presents Fast Geometric Projections (FGP), an algorithm for certifying local robustness of neural networks, particularly for ℓ₂ norms. The method uses geometric projections to search activation regions around an input point, avoiding expensive constraint solving. The authors claim FGP is orders of magnitude faster than exact verifiers while maintaining precision, and provides tighter bounds than approximate methods.

The paper addresses an important gap in ℓ₂ robustness verification with a clever geometric approach. The empirical results showing 2-4 orders of magnitude speedup over exact methods while maintaining precision are compelling. The algorithm design is elegant and the GPU implementation is well-motivated. However, the incompleteness of the method and its handling of ambiguous cases require deeper analysis, and the comparison to approximate methods on large models could be strengthened.

Overview issues:

- Incompleteness analysis underestimates practical impact: The paper acknowledges FGP is incomplete but downplays this limitation. The 'unknown' cases occur precisely when the projection onto a decision boundary lies outside the current activation region (Figure 1b). The authors show a heuristic (exploring full queue) reduces unknowns by ~50%, but this is insufficient. For ℓ∞ norm, Table 3 shows up to 8% unknowns even on small networks. The paper should analyze: (1) What network characteristics lead to unknowns? (2) How does the unknown rate scale with network depth/width? (3) Can we bound the worst-case number of unknowns? Without this analysis, practitioners cannot determine when FGP will be reliable.

- Missing theoretical analysis of projection quality: The core algorithmic innovation is using projections to hyperplanes instead of exact polytope distances. While this yields efficiency gains, the paper lacks theoretical analysis of how this approximation affects completeness. Specifically: (1) What is the relationship between the hyperplane distance and true polytope distance? (2) Under what conditions does the hyperplane projection equal the true projection? (3) Can we bound the gap between these distances? The authors state hyperplane distance is a lower bound (Section 2.1), but no analysis of tightness is provided. This is crucial for understanding when FGP will succeed.

- Insufficient comparison with approximate methods on large models: The comparison to FastLin in Table 2(b) uses relatively small models (max 1000 neurons) and reports only mean bounds. For large models where FGP doesn't terminate quickly, we need to understand the trade-off. The paper should include: (1) Runtime vs. bound quality curves for both methods on large networks; (2) Comparison on models where FGP times out; (3) Analysis of when FastLin's bounds are 'too conservative' in practice. Without this, the claim that FGP provides 'tighter bounds' is not fully substantiated for the large-scale setting where approximate methods matter most.

- Limited analysis of CNN scalability: The paper claims FGP scales to CNNs (Section 3.1) but only evaluates one CNN architecture on CIFAR-10. The results show 86% robust accuracy with ε=0.1, but: (1) No comparison to other ℓ₂ CNN verifiers (if any exist); (2) No analysis of how performance scales with CNN depth/width; (3) No discussion of convolution-specific optimizations. Given CNNs are the dominant architecture in practice, more thorough evaluation is needed to support the scalability claim. The single CNN result appears more like a proof-of-concept than a comprehensive evaluation.

- Unclear handling of box constraints in practice: Section 2.1 mentions box constraints can be incorporated by checking them when finding a decision boundary, but this is superficial. For image inputs: (1) How often do projections violate [0,1] constraints? (2) Does checking these constraints significantly increase unknown cases? (3) Are there efficient ways to incorporate box constraints into the projection itself? The empirical evaluation should report the frequency of box constraint violations and their impact on certification results. Without this, the practical applicability to real image domains is unclear.

- Missing worked example of algorithm execution: The paper would benefit from a concrete, step-by-step walkthrough of the FGP algorithm on a simple network. For instance, consider a 2-input ReLU network with one hidden layer of 2 neurons. The authors should compute the activation constraints for the region containing a test point, show the projection calculations onto decision boundaries, and demonstrate how the queue evolves. This would help readers understand the mechanics of the algorithm and clarify the cases where projections fall outside activation regions (Figure 1b). The current description is abstract, making it difficult to verify the algorithm's behavior without implementing it.

- No demonstration of certified lower bound tightness: The paper claims the certified lower bounds are 'consistently tighter' and 'in some cases minimal' (Section 3.2), but provides only aggregate statistics. A concrete example showing minimal tightness would strengthen this claim. The authors should select a network instance where FGP finds a decision boundary and computes the exact robustness radius, then compare this to FastLin's bound on the same instance. This would illustrate the magnitude of FastLin's conservativeness and demonstrate FGP's ability to achieve optimal bounds. The current Table 2(b) only shows averages, which doesn't convey the practical significance of the improvement.

- Missing analysis of unknown case frequency: While the paper reports unknown rates in Tables 2 and 3, it lacks analysis of what network characteristics cause these cases. The authors should examine whether unknowns correlate with: (1) distance of x to decision boundaries, (2) number of activation constraints near x, (3) network depth/width. A scatter plot showing unknown rate vs. these metrics across multiple networks would help practitioners predict when FGP will be reliable. Without this analysis, the heuristic of exploring the full queue (Section 2.2) appears ad hoc, and readers cannot assess whether the ~50% reduction in unknowns will generalize to their models.

- Insufficient guidance on hyperparameter selection: The implementation mentions batching heuristics (Section 2.2) with batch sizes of 10-100, but provides no guidance on selecting these values. The paper should analyze how batch size affects runtime and memory usage across different network architectures. For example, a plot showing speedup vs. batch size for models of varying width would help practitioners tune their implementations. Additionally, the priority queue variant for lower bounds (Section 2.3) needs guidance on when to terminate early versus waiting for full completion. Without this implementation guidance, reproducing the reported performance gains may be difficult.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide theoretical analysis of the approximation gap between hyperplane and polytope projections, including bounds on when they coincide

- Analyze the characteristics and frequency of 'unknown' cases across different network architectures and sizes, with scaling studies

- Expand comparison with approximate methods to include large models where FGP doesn't terminate, showing runtime vs. bound quality trade-offs

- Add more comprehensive CNN evaluation with multiple architectures and scaling analysis

- Report empirical data on box constraint violations and their impact on certification results

comments.title: Gradient claim needs boundary qualification
comments.quote: The coefficients,  $w_u$  are equal to the gradient of  $f_u$  with respect to its inputs, evaluated at a point in  $\bar{A}$ . Crucially, because the gradient is the same at every point in  $\bar{A}$ , the constraints can be computed from the activation pattern alone via backpropagation.
comments.explanation: The claim that the gradient is the same at every point in $\bar{A}$ is only true for the interior of the activation region, not on boundaries where $f_u(x) = 0$. The gradient is undefined at these boundary points. This should be qualified: "The coefficients, $w_u$, are equal to the gradient of $f_u$ with respect to its inputs, evaluated at any point in the interior of $\bar{A}$. Crucially, because the gradient is constant throughout the interior of $\bar{A}$, the constraints can be computed from the activation pattern alone via backpropagation."

comments.title: Missing justification for distance bound claim
comments.quote: Although $C_{u}$ is only valid in the polytope corresponding to the activation region $\bar{A}_{0}$, we compute the distance to the hyperplane corresponding to the infinite prolongation of $C_{u}$. As such, we only obtain a lower bound on the distance from $x$ to $C_{u}$.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that computing distance to the infinite prolongation of $C_u$ yields a lower bound on the distance to the actual constraint within $\bar{A}_0$, but provides no justification. This is not always true: if $x$ is outside the polytope, the closest point on the infinite hyperplane could be closer than any point on the finite constraint segment within $\bar{A}_0$. Add justification: "Since $C_u$ is a subset of its infinite prolongation, the distance to the prolongation is less than or equal to the distance to $C_u$, providing a lower bound."

comments.title: Algorithmic handling of box constraints is incomplete
comments.quote: These constraints can easily be incorporated into the FGP algorithm by checking them when we check if $F(x)\neq F(p)$, when a decision boundary is found. If $p$ is not a valid adversarial example, the algorithm would return unknown.
comments.explanation: The description of handling box constraints is problematic because it suggests the algorithm simply returns 'unknown' when a projected point violates box constraints. This approach would significantly increase the unknown rate and undermine the algorithm's practical utility. A more robust approach would be to project the point onto the feasible region (the intersection of the ℓ₂ ball and box constraints) or to continue searching for other decision boundaries within the feasible region. The current description makes the algorithm sound brittle - a single projection outside [0,1] would cause failure even when valid adversarial examples exist within the ε-ball. This contradicts the paper's claim of practical effectiveness on image domains where box constraints are essential.

comments.title: Loop invariant condition (1) insufficiently precise
comments.quote: Loop Invariant 1. (1) All activation regions at distance less than  $\beta$  from  $x$  were previously visited, (2)  $\beta$  is always smaller than  $\epsilon$ , and (3) there is no adversarial point at distance less than  $\beta$ .
comments.explanation: The algorithm processes constraints (hyperplanes) using a priority queue based on distance from x, but condition (1) refers to activation regions. The distance from x to a constraint (hyperplane) can differ significantly from the distance to the corresponding activation region. For example, a constraint might be at distance 0.1 from x while the activation region it bounds could be at distance 1.0. Since the algorithm only guarantees processing of constraints up to distance β, the invariant should reference constraints, not regions. Rewrite condition (1) as 'All constraints at distance less than β from x were previously visited' to match the algorithm's actual processing.

comments.title: Loop invariant condition (2) contradicts termination case
comments.quote: The algorithm terminates either when all constraints at distance less than the initially specified  $\epsilon$  were handled, or when a decision boundary for which the initial algorithm returns unknown or not_robust is found. In the first case, we return  $\epsilon$ ; in the second, we return the value stored in  $\beta$  at this iteration.
comments.explanation: If all constraints at distance less than ε are processed, β would be updated to the maximum distance among these constraints. If a constraint exists exactly at distance ε (which is allowed since the condition is 'less than ε'), then β could equal ε, violating condition (2) that β < ε. The invariant should state β ≤ ε to be consistent with the termination condition. Alternatively, the algorithm should return min(β, ε) in the first termination case to maintain consistency.

comments.title: Missing VRA calculation for cifar-cnn model
comments.explanation: For the cifar-cnn model, the VRA is reported as 0.27, but this appears inconsistent with the raw counts. With 86 robust (R) and 14 not_robust (NR) cases out of 100 instances, the VRA should be 86/100 = 0.86, not 0.27. The 0.27 value appears to be the VRAUB (upper bound) instead. This is a critical error that misrepresents the certified robust accuracy. The VRA column should show 0.86, and the current 0.27 should be moved to the VRAUB column if it represents the upper bound.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated VRA upper bound claim
comments.quote: In addition, we report an upper bound on the VRA for each model, obtained by running PGD attacks (hyperparameters included in Appendix C) on the correctly-classified points on which every method either timed out or reported unknown.
comments.explanation: The claim that PGD attacks provide an upper bound on VRA is incorrect. PGD is a heuristic attack method that may fail to find existing adversarial examples, so it provides only a lower bound on the true adversarial risk. The relationship should be: VRA ≤ 1 - (successful PGD attack rate), but since PGD can miss attacks, this gives only a loose estimate of the upper bound. Rewrite the sentence as: 'In addition, we report a loose upper bound on the VRA for each model, obtained by running PGD attacks... though this bound may not be tight due to PGD's inability to guarantee finding all adversarial examples.'

comments.title: Inconsistent timeout comparison claim
comments.quote: MIP frequently times out with a time budget of 120 seconds, indicating that we are faster by at least four orders of magnitude. This is consistent with the 100 to 1000 seconds per solve on a MNIST model with three hidden layers of 20 neurons each reported by Tjeng & Tedrake (2017); their technique performs best on the  $\ell_1$  or  $\ell_{\infty}$
comments.explanation: The 'four orders of magnitude' speedup claim is mathematically inconsistent. If MIP times out at 120 seconds, the maximum possible speedup is 120 divided by FGP's runtime. For a 10,000x speedup, FGP would need to complete in 0.012 seconds, which is unrealistic. The cited reference (100-1000 seconds) would only support at most a 1-2 order magnitude improvement. Remove the 'four orders of magnitude' claim or provide concrete runtime data to support it.

comments.title: Volume comparison between ℓ∞ and ℓ₂ balls
comments.quote: Results are given in Table 3 for an $\ell_{\infty}$ norm bound of $\epsilon=0.01$, which contains roughly the same volume as the $\ell_{2}$ ball used in most of our evaluation.
comments.explanation:
The volume comparison is incorrect. For n-dimensional space, vol(ℓ∞ ball) = (2ε)^n and vol(ℓ₂ ball) = π^(n/2)/Γ(n/2+1) * ε^n. For MNIST (n=784) with ε∞=0.01 and ε₂=0.25 (from Section 3.1), the volumes are (0.02)^784 ≈ 10^(-1080) versus π^(392)/Γ(393) * 0.25^784 ≈ 10^(-50). These differ by ~10^1030, not 'roughly the same'.

Rewrite the sentence as: 'Results are given in Table 3 for an $\ell_{\infty}$ norm bound of $\epsilon=0.01$, which is a standard perturbation budget for $\ell_{\infty}$ robustness evaluation.'

comments.title: Incorrect citation attribution for FastLin
comments.quote: FastLin *(Weng et al., 2018)* exploits the special structure of ReLU networks to efficiently compute lower bounds on minimal adversarial distortions. CROWN *(Zhang et al., 2018)* later expanded this to general activation functions.
comments.explanation: The citation for FastLin is incorrect. FastLin was introduced by Huan Zhang, Hongge Chen, Duane Boning, and Cho-Jui Hsieh in their 2018 NeurIPS workshop paper, not by Weng et al. (2018). Weng et al. (2018) introduced PROVEN, which is a probabilistic certification framework. This error misattributes the foundational work on linear bounds for ReLU networks.

comments.title: Overbroad efficiency claim without qualification
comments.quote: Empirically we find this approach to be far more precise than many approximate verification approaches, while at the same time performing multiple orders of magnitude faster than complete verifiers, and scaling to much deeper networks.
comments.explanation: The claim of 'multiple orders of magnitude faster than complete verifiers' is too strong without qualification. The paper's own results (Table 2) show timeouts (TO) occurring even on relatively small networks, which contradicts the implication of universal speedup. The claim should be qualified to reflect the conditions under which this speedup holds, such as 'when the algorithm terminates' or 'on networks where it completes within the time budget'.

comments.title: Inconsistent runtime reporting in Table 1
comments.explanation: The text claims the full queue heuristic has 'only having a minor impact on the speed of the execution' and 'up to 2x speedup' from batching, but Table 1 shows significant runtime increases for larger models. For mnist20x9, runtime increases from 4.897s to 7.389s (51% increase), and for mnist40x3, from 5.417s to 6.712s (24% increase). This contradicts the claim of minor speed impact. The authors should either qualify their claim to apply only to smaller models or explain why these increases are considered 'minor'.

comments.title: Unverified claim about optimal bound tightness
comments.quote: Finally, we note that when FGP terminates by finding a decision boundary, if the projection onto that boundary is a true adversarial example, then *the lower bound is tight*.
comments.explanation: The claim that the lower bound is tight when FGP finds a decision boundary with a true adversarial example requires mathematical justification. The paper states this as a fact but provides no proof or reference. For the bound to be tight, the projection distance must equal the minimum distance to any adversarial example within the norm ball. However, FGP projects onto hyperplanes representing decision boundaries, not necessarily the closest point on the actual decision boundary. The authors need to prove that when the projected point is adversarial, it represents the minimum distance adversarial example, or provide a theorem establishing this property. Add a theorem statement and proof after the quoted text to establish that when FGP terminates with a true adversarial example, the projection distance equals the minimum adversarial distance.

comments.title: Inconsistent time comparison methodology
comments.quote: We compare the certified lower bound reported by our implementation of FGP after 60 seconds of computation (on models large enough such that FGP rarely terminates in 60 seconds) to the lower bound reported by FastLin; FastLin always reported results in less than two seconds on the models analyzed.
comments.explanation: The comparison methodology is problematic because FGP is given a fixed 60-second budget while FastLin is allowed to run to completion (which takes <2 seconds). This creates an unfair comparison where FGP may be artificially constrained. For a valid comparison, either both methods should be given the same time budget, or FGP should be allowed to run to completion. The current setup doesn't distinguish whether FGP's worse performance is due to algorithmic limitations or the artificial time constraint. Rewrite the quoted text as 'We compare the certified lower bound reported by our implementation of FGP after 60 seconds of computation to the lower bound reported by FastLin after the same 60-second time budget; FastLin always converged in less than two seconds on the models analyzed, leaving the remaining time unused' to ensure fair comparison methodology.

comments.title: Missing ℓ∞ projection algorithm details
comments.quote: As such, we added support for $\ell_{\infty}$ robustness certification by providing a projection and a projected distance computation in FGP.
comments.explanation:
The paper states they provide ℓ∞ projection but doesn't specify the algorithm. For ℓ∞ projection onto a hyperplane {x: w^T x = b}, the solution involves clamping components but requires handling the case where the clamped point doesn't satisfy the constraint. Without specifying how they resolve this (e.g., iterative adjustment), readers cannot verify correctness or reproduce results.

Add after this sentence: 'Our ℓ∞ projection first clamps each coordinate to [x_i-ε, x_i+ε], then iteratively adjusts coordinates violating the hyperplane constraint using a water-filling algorithm until convergence.'

comments.title: Non-unique ℓ∞ projections and adversarial examples
comments.quote: While the projection in Euclidean space is unique, this is not always the case in  $\ell_{\infty}$  space; we pick one arbitrary projection which thus might not lead to an adversarial example.
comments.explanation:
The explanation is incomplete. In ℓ∞ space, projecting onto a hyperplane can yield a face of the ℓ∞ ball (dimension > 0). The paper mentions picking 'one arbitrary projection' but doesn't specify the selection rule or whether multiple projections are explored when the initial choice fails. This affects completeness: some projections may lie outside the current activation region while others inside it.

Add after this sentence: 'In our implementation, when the initial projection fails to yield an adversarial example, we explore the extreme points of the projection face to maximize the chance of finding a valid adversarial direction.'

comments.title: Overstated precision claim
comments.quote: Our implementation outperforms existing exact tools by multiple orders of magnitude, while empirically maintaining the same or better precision under a time constraint.
comments.explanation: The paper's own results in Table 2 show that FGP produces 'unknown' outcomes in 3-8% of cases even on small networks, which represents a precision limitation compared to exact tools that always terminate with a definitive answer. The claim of 'same or better precision' is inaccurate because exact tools have perfect precision (they never produce unknown results), while FGP's precision is limited by its incompleteness. The empirical results show FGP is faster but less precise than exact tools.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated scaling claim
comments.quote: Additionally, we show that our approach is particularly suited to scale up network verification to *deeper* networks—a promising step towards verifying large, state-of-the-art models.
comments.explanation: The paper only evaluates scaling on fully connected networks up to 6 layers (Table 2) and provides a single CNN result on CIFAR-10. There is no empirical evidence demonstrating that FGP is 'particularly suited' for deeper networks compared to other methods. The evaluation lacks systematic analysis of how performance scales with depth, and no comparison to state-of-the-art models is provided. This claim overstates what the paper actually demonstrates.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in activation constraint
comments.quote: The activation constraint,  $C_u$ , for neuron  $u$  and pattern  $A$  is the linear inequality  $w_u^T x + b_u \leq 0$ , where  $w_u$  and  $b_u$  satisfy  $\forall x \in \mathbb{R}^m$ .  $w_u^T x + b \leq 0 \iff A(u)$ . The coefficients,  $w_u$  are equal to the gradient of  $f_u$  with respect to its inputs, evaluated at a point in  $\bar{A}$ . Crucially, because the gradient is the same at every point in  $\bar{A}$ , the constraints can be computed from the activation pattern alone via backpropagation.
comments.explanation: The text contains a notation inconsistency where $b_u$ is used in the constraint definition but $b$ (without subscript) appears in the equivalence condition. This creates ambiguity about whether these refer to the same quantity. Fix the notation to be consistent: "The activation constraint, $C_u$, for neuron $u$ and pattern $A$ is the linear inequality $w_u^T x + b_u \leq 0$, where $w_u$ and $b_u$ satisfy $\forall x \in \mathbb{R}^m: w_u^T x + b_u \leq 0 \iff A(u)$."

comments.title: Undefined dagger symbol in table
comments.explanation: The cifar-cnn model entry includes a dagger symbol (†) that is not defined in the table caption or surrounding text. The text explains that * indicates MMR training and + indicates RS training, but provides no explanation for the dagger symbol. This creates ambiguity about what distinguishes this cifar-cnn model from others. Add a note in the caption: "† indicates a model trained with standard PGD adversarial training" or remove the symbol if it's not meaningful.

comments.title: Inconsistent epsilon values across tables
comments.quote: Results are for  $\epsilon = 0.25$ , except on CIFAR10, where we use  $\epsilon = 0.1$ , as was used by (Croce et al., 2019). (b) Comparison of the lower bound obtained by FGP (Section 2.3) to that obtained by FastLin.
